Conditions A to Z
Craniosynostosis 
Craniosynostosis is a rare condition where a baby's skull does not grow properly and their head becomes an unusual shape. It's usually treated with surgery. 
Symptoms of craniosynostosis 
Babies' heads come in all shapes and sizes. It's normal for their head to be a slightly unusual shape. It will often get better as they grow.
But your baby may have a problem like craniosynostosis if:
their head is long and narrow, like a rugby ball their forehead is pointy or triangular one side of their head is flattened or bulging out the soft spot on the top of their head (fontanelle) disappears before they're 1 year old If the problem is mild, it may not be noticeable until your child is older.
Rarely, your child may have symptoms such as:
learning difficulties sight or hearing loss breathing problems, like not being able to breathe through their mouth dental problems, such as missing or crowded teeth 
Non-urgent advice: See a GP if:
Your baby or child has symptoms of craniosynostosis and:
they seem to have problems with their vision or hearing they have speech problems or they're slow to start talking they have difficulty breathing nor
